subtopic_detail: Hyperkalaemia with ECG changes - first-line cardioprotection
stem: You are the medical registrar in a busy emergency department called by the resuscitation nurse with a critical potassium result on a 69-year-old man with stage 4 chronic kidney disease (baseline eGFR 22) who presented 2 hours ago with worsening lethargy and reduced urine output for 3 days. He takes perindopril 8 mg, spironolactone 25 mg and frusemide 80 mg daily. He started a high-dose oral potassium chloride supplement from a friend last week. He has no chest pain. Routine bloods are back.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 138/82, HR 48 bradycardic, RR 16, SpO2 98% air, T 36.6
data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable, normal cardiovascular exam apart from bradycardia, no peripheral oedema, mild abdominal discomfort, no flank tenderness
data_table.Investigations: K 7.4 mmol/L, Na 136, urea 28, creatinine 280 micromol/L (baseline 220), bicarbonate 18, calcium 2.18, glucose 6.0; ECG: peaked T waves, broad QRS to 140 ms, P waves attenuated

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Insert a vascular dialysis catheter and arrange urgent haemodialysis as the sole initial intervention because pharmacological measures are unreliable in chronic kidney disease and only dialysis removes the potassium load definitively
options.rationale: Dialysis is appropriate for refractory or severe hyperkalaemia but is preceded by IV calcium and insulin-dextrose for immediate cardioprotection while access is being arranged. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Administer 30 g of oral sodium polystyrene sulfonate (resonium) as first-line therapy because it removes potassium definitively over the next 2 hours and obviates the need for intravenous calcium or insulin-dextrose shift therapy
options.rationale: Resonium has slow onset (hours) and no role in immediate cardioprotection; with ECG changes and K above 6.5, IV calcium and insulin-dextrose are first. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Commence intravenous frusemide 80 mg bolus and 1000 mL of 5% dextrose over 2 hours as initial therapy because forced diuresis with isotonic dextrose acts as both potassium shift and excretion in this oliguric kidney patient
options.rationale: Frusemide may help if there is preserved diuresis but is not first-line cardioprotection; with ECG changes IV calcium is the priority. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Administer 50 mL of 8.4% sodium bicarbonate as a slow intravenous push as first-line therapy because acidosis-correction shifts potassium intracellularly more rapidly than any other available pharmacological measure
options.rationale: Bicarbonate is an adjunct in significant acidosis and is slower than insulin-dextrose for shifting; not first-line monotherapy in this scenario. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyperkalaemia)

options.text: Administer 10 mL of 10% calcium gluconate intravenously over 2 to 5 minutes for cardiac membrane stabilisation, then 10 units of intravenous short-acting insulin in 50 mL of 50% dextrose to shift potassium intracellularly, with continuous cardiac monitoring
options.rationale: ECG changes with K above 6.5 mmol/L require immediate calcium gluconate for cardiac stabilisation, followed by insulin-dextrose shift therapy and ongoing nebulised salbutamol and definitive potassium removal per Therapeutic Guidelines and KHA-CARI. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Endocrinology - Hyperkalaemia)

explanation.summary: Hyperkalaemia with ECG changes (peaked T, prolonged PR, wide QRS, sine wave) is a life-threatening emergency. Management priorities are membrane stabilisation, intracellular shift, removal and prevention. First-line: 10 mL of 10% calcium gluconate (or 5 mL of 10% calcium chloride via central line) over 2 to 5 minutes - effect within minutes, lasts 30 to 60 minutes, repeat as needed. Shift: 10 units short-acting insulin in 50 mL of 50% dextrose IV over 10 minutes plus nebulised salbutamol 10 to 20 mg. Removal: loop diuretic if making urine, oral or rectal cation exchange resin (resonium or patiromer), urgent haemodialysis for refractory or severe disease. Stop ACE inhibitor, MRA, K supplements, NSAIDs. Manage cause (AKI, rhabdomyolysis, adrenal insufficiency).
key_points: ECG changes + K over 6.5 = immediate IV calcium
Calcium stabilises membrane in minutes
Insulin-dextrose 10 U with 50 mL 50% dextrose for shift
Salbutamol nebs adjunct shift
Definitive removal: resonium, diuretic, dialysis
explanation.pearls: Calcium chloride 5 mL of 10% has 3 times the elemental calcium of calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% but requires central venous access due to vesicant risk - gluconate is the safer peripheral option.

subtopic_detail: Necrotising soft tissue infection recognition and management
stem: You are the medical registrar reviewing a 58-year-old man with type 2 diabetes (HbA1c 8.9 percent) admitted overnight with a left leg cellulitis from a minor garden injury 3 days ago. Despite intravenous flucloxacillin he has clinically worsened over 8 hours with pain disproportionate to the visible erythema, dusky discolouration extending beyond the marked margin, and new dark haemorrhagic blisters along the medial calf. He is more tachycardic, looks toxic and confused. He has had two intravenous fluid boluses without improvement.

data_table.Vital signs: T 38.4, HR 132, BP 92/58, RR 28, SpO2 95% on 2 L/min, GCS 14, lactate 4.6
data_table.Examination: Confused but rousable, left medial calf swollen tense skin with dusky purpuric discolouration spreading beyond marked margin, two new haemorrhagic bullae, marked tenderness disproportionate to appearance, decreased sensation centrally over the lesion, crepitus on palpation
data_table.Investigations: WCC 22.4 x10^9/L (neutrophils 19.8), Hb 122, platelets 165, Na 132, glucose 16.2 mmol/L, creatinine 162, CRP 380 mg/L, lactate 4.6 mmol/L, INR 1.4, LRINEC score calculated as 9

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Continue intravenous flucloxacillin at the current dose, add intravenous gentamicin 5 mg/kg daily and arrange routine elective surgical review on the next routine list in 24 to 48 hours pending repeat blood cultures and clinical reassessment
options.rationale: Necrotising fasciitis demands urgent (not elective) surgical debridement and broader antibiotic cover; flucloxacillin alone misses Gram-negatives, anaerobes and streptococcal toxin suppression. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Necrotising fasciitis)

options.text: Initiate sepsis bundle with intravenous fluid resuscitation, blood cultures, broad-spectrum intravenous meropenem plus clindamycin plus vancomycin and arrange immediate surgical consultation for urgent operative debridement in theatre
options.rationale: LRINEC at or above 8 with clinical features (pain disproportionate, dusky discolouration, bullae, crepitus, toxicity) is necrotising fasciitis until proven otherwise; broad-spectrum cover (carbapenem + clindamycin for toxin suppression + vanco for MRSA) and urgent surgical debridement save life. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Necrotising fasciitis)

options.text: Arrange urgent magnetic resonance imaging of the limb to confirm necrotising fasciitis before any change in antibiotic therapy or surgical decision making because imaging is required to distinguish soft tissue infection from cellulitis
options.rationale: Imaging may help but never delays surgical debridement in suspected nec fasc; the diagnosis is clinical and surgical findings are confirmatory. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Necrotising fasciitis)

options.text: Apply a topical silver sulfadiazine dressing to the bullae, mark the erythema margins with a black pen and continue intravenous flucloxacillin while observing for response over the next 12 hours of inpatient management
options.rationale: Topical management is inappropriate for necrotising fasciitis; this strategy will result in rapid progression and death without surgical debridement. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Necrotising fasciitis)

options.text: Commence intravenous immunoglobulin 1 g/kg as monotherapy for streptococcal toxic shock syndrome and observe for 12 hours before considering antibiotic broadening or surgical referral if no clinical improvement has occurred
options.rationale: IVIG may be adjunctive in severe group A streptococcal toxic shock but is not monotherapy and does not replace surgery and broad-spectrum antibiotics. (Source: Therapeutic Guidelines: Antibiotic - Necrotising fasciitis)

explanation.summary: Necrotising soft tissue infection (NSTI) is a surgical emergency with mortality 20 to 40 percent. Recognise by pain disproportionate to appearance, rapid progression, dusky or violaceous discolouration, haemorrhagic bullae, crepitus, anaesthesia of overlying skin and systemic toxicity. LRINEC score at or above 6 raises suspicion (CRP, WCC, Hb, Na, creatinine, glucose), at or above 8 is high probability. Diabetes, immunosuppression and IV drug use are risk factors. Management is urgent surgical debridement (the cornerstone), broad-spectrum antibiotics covering Gram-positives (including MRSA), Gram-negatives and anaerobes (meropenem plus vancomycin plus clindamycin per Australian guidelines), aggressive ICU-level resuscitation, and consideration of IVIG for streptococcal toxic shock syndrome.
key_points: Pain disproportionate to appearance + bullae + crepitus + toxicity = NSTI
LRINEC score helps risk-stratify but never delays surgery
Urgent surgical debridement is the cornerstone
Empirical: meropenem + clindamycin (toxin suppression) + vancomycin
ICU support, IVIG for documented strep TSS
explanation.pearls: Clindamycin is added not for its antibacterial spectrum but to suppress streptococcal exotoxin production (Eagle effect) - it is included in regimens regardless of the primary antibiotic choice.

sources.label: Surgical Infection Society guideline: Skin and soft tissue infection

subtopic_detail: Septic shock hour-1 bundle and vasopressor selection
stem: You are the medical registrar in the resuscitation area reviewing a 64-year-old woman with type 2 diabetes admitted with dysuria, frank haematuria and right loin pain progressing over 36 hours. On arrival she is confused, peripherally cool with prolonged capillary refill, and breathing rapidly. She has received 30 mL/kg of intravenous Hartmann's over 90 minutes without improvement. Blood cultures and a urine culture were obtained on arrival. She has been given intravenous ceftriaxone and gentamicin.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 78/40, MAP 53, HR 132 sinus, RR 32, T 39.2, SpO2 96% on 4 L/min, GCS 14, lactate 5.4
data_table.Examination: Confused, cool peripheries, mottled knees, right renal angle tender, no abdominal peritonism, no rash
data_table.Investigations: WCC 19.8 (neutrophils 17.0), CRP 240, creatinine 192 (baseline 88), Na 130, K 4.6, glucose 16.4, ABG pH 7.28 pCO2 30 pO2 90 HCO3 16 BE -8, lactate 5.4 mmol/L, urine dipstick 3+ blood 3+ leucocytes 2+ nitrites

lead_in: What is the most appropriate next step?

options.text: Administer a further 30 mL/kg of intravenous 0.9% sodium chloride over 30 minutes and reassess MAP before considering vasopressors, because all sepsis patients need at least 60 mL/kg of resuscitation fluid in the first hour to maximise preload
options.rationale: After 30 mL/kg of fluid with persistent shock, vasopressors are started; further crystalloid risks fluid overload and pulmonary oedema. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Commence an intravenous noradrenaline infusion via peripheral cannula at 0.05 mcg/kg/min targeting a mean arterial pressure of at least 65 mmHg, arrange immediate intensive care review, repeat serum lactate at 2 hours and image the renal tract for obstruction
options.rationale: Septic shock with persistent hypotension after 30 mL/kg fluid is the indication for early vasopressor (noradrenaline first-line) with MAP target of at least 65 mmHg, ICU referral and source control investigation (urinary tract imaging) per the Surviving Sepsis bundle and Therapeutic Guidelines Antibiotic. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Commence intravenous adrenaline 1 mcg/kg/min as first-line vasoactive therapy for septic shock because adrenaline provides both inotropic and vasopressor support and is recommended ahead of noradrenaline in current Australian intensive care guidelines
options.rationale: Noradrenaline is first-line per Surviving Sepsis and ANZICS; adrenaline is added if MAP is inadequate on noradrenaline plus vasopressin. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Administer intravenous hydrocortisone 200 mg as initial monotherapy for refractory septic shock before commencing any vasopressor therapy because corticosteroids alone often reverse hypotension by restoring vascular tone
options.rationale: Hydrocortisone (200 mg/day) is added only when MAP is inadequate despite escalating vasopressors; it is not first-line nor monotherapy for septic shock. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

options.text: Switch antibiotic therapy to piperacillin tazobactam 4.5 g intravenously and meropenem 1 g intravenously as combination Gram-negative cover and observe response over 4 hours before any vasopressor or further fluid resuscitation is started
options.rationale: Antibiotic choice should be optimised but vasopressors cannot be delayed; double Gram-negative cover is not routinely required in community-acquired urinary sepsis. (Source: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021)

explanation.summary: Sepsis hour-1 bundle: measure lactate, obtain blood cultures, give broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour, give 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate at or above 4 mmol/L, and start vasopressors if hypotensive during or after fluid to maintain MAP at or above 65 mmHg. Noradrenaline is first-line vasopressor (peripheral acceptable up to 0.2 mcg/kg/min as a bridge to central). Vasopressin 0.03 U/min is added if noradrenaline above 0.25 mcg/kg/min. Hydrocortisone 200 mg/day is added when MAP is inadequate despite noradrenaline plus vasopressin. Source control (drain, debride, decompress) is sought urgently. Reassess lactate at 2 hours and clinical response.
key_points: Hour-1 bundle: lactate, cultures, antibiotics, fluid, vasopressors
30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate over 4
Noradrenaline first-line; vasopressin add-on, adrenaline rescue
Hydrocortisone for refractory shock on dual vasopressor
Source control is critical
explanation.pearls: Peripheral noradrenaline infusion is safe up to 0.2 mcg/kg/min via a proximal antecubital vein for short periods (under 6 hours) - this avoids delaying haemodynamic support while central access is being secured.

subtopic_detail: Acute type A aortic dissection diagnosis and BP control
stem: You are the medical registrar in resuscitation reviewing a 62-year-old man with poorly controlled hypertension (home BP often 170/100) and a 30 pack-year smoking history who presents with sudden onset severe tearing chest pain radiating through to the interscapular area, starting 40 minutes ago. He looks pale and diaphoretic. On examination there is an early diastolic murmur at the left sternal edge new since last documented review, a 25 mmHg differential between his right and left arms, and weak right radial pulse. ECG shows sinus tachycardia and old left ventricular hypertrophy. Bedside chest X-ray shows a widened mediastinum.

data_table.Vital signs: BP right arm 172/108, left arm 144/86, HR 108, RR 20, SpO2 97% air, T 36.6
data_table.Examination: Diaphoretic, soft early diastolic murmur left sternal edge, weak right radial pulse, normal left radial pulse, no focal neurology, abdomen soft, no leg pulse asymmetry
data_table.Investigations: Troponin marginally raised at 78 ng/L (URL 14), creatinine 102, INR 1.0, lactate 1.8; ECG sinus tachycardia LVH no acute ST changes; CXR widened mediastinum

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Refer to the catheter lab for urgent invasive coronary angiography under the suspicion of left main acute coronary syndrome because dissection rarely causes a 25 mmHg interarm differential and the murmur is more likely chronic
options.rationale: Angiography risks instrument-induced dissection extension; CTA aorta is the imaging of choice when dissection is suspected. (Source: National Heart Foundation: Acute aortic syndromes)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation: Acute aortic syndromes

options.text: Administer 300 mg of aspirin orally and 600 mg of clopidogrel as a dual antiplatelet load and arrange urgent coronary angiography because the marginally elevated troponin and chest pain are most consistent with acute coronary syndrome
options.rationale: DAPT in aortic dissection causes catastrophic bleeding on aortic repair; the dissection picture (tearing pain, pulse differential, widened mediastinum, AR murmur) trumps the marginal troponin. (Source: National Heart Foundation: Acute aortic syndromes)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation: Acute aortic syndromes

options.text: Commence an intravenous nitroglycerin infusion at 50 mcg/min as initial monotherapy aiming for blood pressure under 120 mmHg without a beta blocker because vasodilator-first management reduces wall shear stress most rapidly in dissection
options.rationale: Vasodilator without prior beta blockade causes reflex tachycardia which increases wall shear stress and propagates dissection; beta blocker is given first. (Source: National Heart Foundation: Acute aortic syndromes)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation: Acute aortic syndromes

options.text: Administer therapeutic-dose enoxaparin 1 mg/kg twice daily and arrange CT pulmonary angiography urgently for suspected acute pulmonary embolism with right ventricular strain that explains the murmur and asymmetric pulses
options.rationale: PE does not cause pulse differential or AR murmur and anticoagulation is catastrophic in undiagnosed dissection; CTA aorta not CTPA is the correct first imaging. (Source: National Heart Foundation: Acute aortic syndromes)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation: Acute aortic syndromes

options.text: Commence intravenous esmolol or labetalol infusion targeting heart rate 60 to 80 and systolic blood pressure 100 to 120 mmHg, manage pain with intravenous opioid, and arrange urgent CT angiogram of the aorta with cardiothoracic surgical consultation in parallel
options.rationale: Suspected type A aortic dissection mandates immediate beta blockade (rate then BP) to reduce shear stress, opioid analgesia, urgent CT angiography of the aorta and cardiothoracic surgical involvement per Heart Foundation and Therapeutic Guidelines. (Source: National Heart Foundation: Acute aortic syndromes)
source_refs: National Heart Foundation: Acute aortic syndromes

explanation.summary: Acute aortic dissection presents with sudden severe tearing chest or back pain, pulse differential, new aortic regurgitation murmur, neurological signs (carotid involvement), syncope or limb ischaemia. Type A involves the ascending aorta and is a surgical emergency (mortality 1 to 2 percent per hour without surgery). Type B (descending only) is managed medically unless complicated. Initial management is rate-and-pressure control with intravenous beta blocker (esmolol or labetalol) targeting HR 60 to 80 and SBP 100 to 120, then vasodilator (sodium nitroprusside or GTN) added if BP target not met. Opioid analgesia, large-bore IV access, group and crossmatch and urgent CT angiogram of the whole aorta with cardiothoracic surgical consultation are the immediate steps.
key_points: Sudden tearing chest/back pain + pulse differential + AR murmur
CT angiogram of aorta (CTA) is the imaging of choice
Beta blocker FIRST to reduce shear stress, then vasodilator if needed
Target HR 60-80, SBP 100-120
Type A: surgical emergency. Type B: usually medical
explanation.pearls: A normal D-dimer has high negative predictive value (above 95 percent) for aortic dissection in low-pretest probability patients - a useful rule-out tool when CT angiogram is delayed, but never used to rule out in high-risk presentations.

sources.label: National Heart Foundation: Acute aortic syndromes

subtopic_detail: Adrenal crisis with intercurrent illness - immediate management
stem: You are the medical registrar at the resuscitation bay reviewing a 38-year-old woman with known primary autoimmune adrenal insufficiency since age 19 on hydrocortisone 10/5/5 mg and fludrocortisone 100 mcg daily. She has had 36 hours of vomiting from gastroenteritis. She told her mother she could not keep her medications down but did not double-dose with sick-day rules. On arrival she is profoundly hypotensive, drowsy, peripherally cool and has been given 1 L of intravenous Hartmann's en route by paramedics. Her partner is reading her steroid emergency card. The endocrine consultant is being paged.

data_table.Vital signs: BP 72/40, HR 130 sinus, RR 24, T 36.4, SpO2 97% air, GCS 13, BSL 3.4
data_table.Examination: Drowsy but rousable, mucous membranes dry, capillary refill 4 sec, generalised hyperpigmentation including palmar creases, no focal neurology, no abdominal peritonism
data_table.Investigations: Na 124 mmol/L, K 6.2, urea 14.8, creatinine 142, glucose 3.4, calcium corrected 2.42, cortisol on arrival drawn at 0830 hours is 84 nmol/L (subnormal); VBG pH 7.30 HCO3 16, lactate 3.2

lead_in: What is the most appropriate immediate management?

options.text: Administer 100 mg of intravenous hydrocortisone immediately, commence intravenous 0.9% sodium chloride 1 L bolus and 10% dextrose to correct the hypoglycaemia, and start continuous hydrocortisone 200 mg over 24 hours pending source identification of the precipitating illness
options.rationale: Adrenal crisis is a clinical diagnosis: rapid IV hydrocortisone 100 mg + IV saline + dextrose for hypoglycaemia, continue hydrocortisone 200 mg over 24 hours (50 mg q6h or infusion), source-control of precipitant, per Australasian Endocrine Society and Therapeutic Guidelines. (Source: ESA: Management of adrenal insufficiency and adrenal crisis)
source_refs: ESA: Management of adrenal insufficiency and adrenal crisis

options.text: Administer intravenous fludrocortisone 200 mcg as the priority steroid for the mineralocorticoid deficiency and the hyperkalaemia, and defer hydrocortisone until urgent ACTH stimulation testing has confirmed the adrenal pathway is unresponsive
options.rationale: Fludrocortisone is not available IV and is not first-line in crisis; high-dose IV hydrocortisone provides both glucocorticoid and at-dose mineralocorticoid effect. ACTH testing has no place during a crisis. (Source: ESA: Management of adrenal insufficiency and adrenal crisis)
source_refs: ESA: Management of adrenal insufficiency and adrenal crisis

options.text: Administer intravenous dexamethasone 8 mg as the first-line glucocorticoid because dexamethasone does not interfere with subsequent cortisol assays and avoids cross-reactivity in the urgent diagnostic ACTH stimulation testing pathway
options.rationale: Dexamethasone can be used if diagnosis is uncertain and stimulation testing is planned, but in established Addison's hydrocortisone is preferred for its combined gluco- and mineralocorticoid actions and immediate efficacy. (Source: ESA: Management of adrenal insufficiency and adrenal crisis)
source_refs: ESA: Management of adrenal insufficiency and adrenal crisis

options.text: Insert a central venous access catheter and commence intravenous noradrenaline 0.05 mcg/kg/min titrated to mean arterial pressure 65 mmHg as the first-line haemodynamic support with intravenous hydrocortisone added only after stable blood pressure is achieved
options.rationale: Vasopressor without urgent hydrocortisone replacement will fail because vascular tone is corticosteroid-dependent; immediate IV hydrocortisone with fluid resuscitation is first-line. (Source: ESA: Management of adrenal insufficiency and adrenal crisis)
source_refs: ESA: Management of adrenal insufficiency and adrenal crisis

options.text: Administer intravenous calcium gluconate 10 mL of 10% for the hyperkalaemia and intravenous insulin-dextrose for potassium shift while she continues her usual oral hydrocortisone dose once vomiting has settled with intravenous ondansetron antiemetic
options.rationale: Hyperkalaemia of adrenal crisis resolves with IV hydrocortisone and saline; oral hydrocortisone will not be absorbed in shock with vomiting and is not an acceptable substitute. (Source: ESA: Management of adrenal insufficiency and adrenal crisis)
source_refs: ESA: Management of adrenal insufficiency and adrenal crisis

explanation.summary: Adrenal crisis presents with hypotension unresponsive to fluids, hyponatraemia, hyperkalaemia, hypoglycaemia and a known or suspected adrenal insufficiency precipitated by intercurrent illness, surgery, missed steroid doses or trauma. It is a clinical diagnosis - treat immediately and confirm later. Management is intravenous hydrocortisone 100 mg as a bolus, then 200 mg over 24 hours (50 mg every 6 hours or continuous infusion), aggressive isotonic crystalloid resuscitation, dextrose for hypoglycaemia and treatment of the precipitant. Fludrocortisone is unnecessary at hydrocortisone doses above 50 mg/day because the gluco at high dose provides mineralocorticoid activity. Sick-day rules (double oral dose for febrile illness, IM hydrocortisone for vomiting), patient-held steroid emergency card and emergency IM hydrocortisone kit are core education points.
key_points: IV hydrocortisone 100 mg STAT then 200 mg over 24 hours
IV 0.9% saline aggressive resuscitation
IV dextrose for hypoglycaemia
Treat precipitant
Sick-day rules + IM emergency kit + steroid emergency card are lifesaving for prevention
explanation.pearls: A morning cortisol drawn during crisis that is over 550 nmol/L effectively excludes adrenal insufficiency; a value under 100 nmol/L while critically unwell is diagnostic for crisis and treatment cannot wait for short Synacthen testing.

sources.label: ESA: Management of adrenal insufficiency and adrenal crisis
